name: Nechtan
description: Nechtan, a figure of considerable importance in Irish mythology, is primarily associated with water, specifically wells and rivers. He is often depicted as the husband of Boann, the goddess of the River Boyne. Nechtan is considered the keeper of the Well of Wisdom, also known as the Well of Segais, from which all the rivers of the world are said to flow. This well is surrounded by hazel trees, whose nuts contain all knowledge. When the nuts fall into the well, salmon eat them and gain wisdom, making the salmon themselves sources of profound knowledge. Only Nechtan and his cupbearers were permitted to approach the well, highlighting its sacred and dangerous nature. Boann's transgression, when she dared to challenge the well's power, led to the creation of the River Boyne, forever altering the landscape and demonstrating the consequences of disrespecting the power of Nechtan and the well. While not always explicitly portrayed as a powerful warrior or king, Nechtan's influence is undeniable, as he controls the source of life and wisdom for the entire world. His role as the guardian of the Well of Wisdom places him at the center of Irish cosmology and knowledge traditions, making him a pivotal, albeit often understated, figure in the pantheon. He represents the potent, life-giving, and sometimes dangerous forces of nature, particularly the element of water and its connection to knowledge and the divine.
subtitle: Guardian of the Well of Wisdom

keyMyths.title: Boann and the Well
keyMyths.description: Boann challenged the Well of Wisdom, causing it to overflow and creating the River Boyne. This act demonstrated the power of the well and the consequences of disrespecting Nechtan's domain.

keyMyths.title: The Well of Segais
keyMyths.description: The Well of Segais, guarded by Nechtan, is the source of all rivers and wisdom. Hazel nuts fall into the well, feeding salmon that gain knowledge.

keyMyths.title: Nechtan's Cupbearers
keyMyths.description: Only Nechtan and his cupbearers were allowed to approach the Well of Wisdom. This highlights the sacred and restricted nature of the well and its guardianship.
